captions.text: Boy, Yacht Club Games had their shit figured  out, didn't they? The dream indie developer
career path: put out one fucking game  that does gangbusters and then, well,
join a fucking Yacht Club, I suppose. But lest ye  be cast into One Hit Wonderland to forever attend
the Mad Dexys Midnight Runners Tea Party, sooner  or later, you've got to follow that up—I mean,
the beer fridge on your yacht isn't gonna fill  itself. The tricky balance now is to play to your
strengths while avoiding being accused of treading  old ground. So how to do that with Shovel Knight,
a very authentic-seeming, retro-styled 8-bit  game with a core mechanic revolving mostly
around digging? You make Mina the Hollower, a  very authentic-seeming, retro-styled 8-bit game
with a core mechanic revolving mostly around  digging, but now you're a mouse. Fuck it,
right? Everything's better with woodland  creatures. Like that time they remade Cannibal
Holocaust with woodland creatures and called  it Watership Down. I kid, of course. Mina the
Hollower is far from treading old ground. In fact,  it breaks new ground. Quite a lot of new ground.
Every second of the game, you're breaking new  ground, actually. Also, I was kidding about the
Cannibal Holocaust thing as well. I thought  that was clear, but you know how people are.
In contrast to Shovel Knight being a side-on  take on Duck Tales for the NES where you're
a knight who digs stuff, Mina the Hollower  is a top-down take on Link's Awakening for
the Game Boy Color where you're a mouse who  digs stuff. Once again, it must have been an
extraordinary amount of effort to look so  convincingly like it's on an 8-bit system,
and once again, this game could never in a million  years actually be run on said 8-bit system 'cause
the Game Boy Color would need to be about a  foot thicker and with 17 more buttons. Story is,
the eponymous Mina the Hollower gets a  letter from a place called Tenebrous Isle,
where she once built a bunch of power generators  that the people now depend upon, asking her to
do some routine maintenance. So she swiftly  becomes Mina the Follower and heads over there,
but as she's coming in, her ship is wrecked  by a giant kraken, and the island is having
trouble with swarms of monsters and a violent  separatist movement who seem convinced that
Mina's generators and the mysterious ancient power  they run on might be what's causing the monsters.
Surely not. Surely this will be the one decadent  society grown rich and complacent on the use of a
mysterious power they don't fully understand  that WON'T reap an inevitable comeuppance.
Yeah, it's one of those plots that I had  entirely mapped out in my head within the
first five minutes, especially after  I met the lord of the island: a big,
scary, well-dressed lion named Lionel (Mina  the Mouse? Lionel the Lion? What is this,
fucking Fingerbobs?) who assures us that  everything will be just fine and all Mina
has to worry her pretty little head about is  exploring the island to fix the six generators,
after which the sun will come out and we'll have  a tea party and all these environmentalist rebels
will realize they were wrong after... let's say  a reasonable amount of state-sponsored violence.
Right you are, boss man. Or perhaps I should say  FINAL boss man. Fortunately, Mina happens to be an
unstoppable badass warrior mouse on top of being a  jobbing civil engineer, and so we choose a weapon
from the fast one, the more damaging one, or the  one with the longer reach and set off to repair
the generators. Which we can technically do in any  order, but there is an obvious difficulty ramp,
so in practical terms, you're gonna start with  the generator being guarded by snails and bits
of twig and end with the one under the protection  of the ferret Harlem Globetrotters or whatever.
The comparison to Link's Awakening begins and  ends with the look and some of the combat.
The broader vibes are closer to something  like Bloodborne, with the focus on opening
up the world, unlocking shortcuts, finding  spectacular boss fights, and a difficulty
level taking an amount of piss that I would say  is not unmanageable. Call it eight or nine liters:
the sort of amount you could get away with in  a swimming pool but which would be a cause for
concern if your dog produced it. Mina's signature  core gameplay mechanic is her ability to briefly
burrow underground like Bugs Bunny, which  pulls double duty as both a traversal mechanic
and a dodge in combat with a free moment of  invulnerability. Using it as a dodge does require
some adjustment because it's delayed by Mina  needing to do a little hop into the air first,
and if there's an attack screaming towards you,  you need to be already mid-dive to avoid damage,
so the increased challenge comes from having to be  generally more anticipatory. Or alternatively just
get used to popping underground as a response to  basically everything like a fucking Whac-A-Mole.
Sorry, is that racist against woodland creatures?  The basic gameplay clicks when you have adjusted
to always having to dodge about a half-second  before you think you have to dodge, and you're
free to enjoy the nicely realized setting, the  sense of exploration and discovery, the array of
fun characters, and let's call it functional art  that pleasingly twiddles the nipples of nostalgia.
However, Mina the Hollower's problem is  that it's a lot less intuitive than it
thinks it is. It seems to be violently allergic  to tutorials. "Hey! Experiment! Discover things
naturally! We're such good designers you  don't need tutorials! What? You need better
directions than 'somewhere to the southeast?'  Get away from me! Where's my fucking EpiPen?!"
Discovering through experimentation is  all very well when it comes to figuring
out that we move faster while burrowed and  jump further when coming out of a burrow,
but there were multiple occasions when I just  had no marmot masturbating clue where to find
the next area. In desperation, I went online to  find some kind of map, not with much hope since
I was playing a review build, and found nothing  but a random hype TikTok titled something like,
"Here's how Mina the Hollower's areas will be so  well designed you won't even NEED a map!" Well,
I'm sorry to differ, Yacht Club Games, but  not everyone has a great sense of direction
or memory for where we've already been. I mean,  I've been known to get confused and turned around
reversing out of my fucking driveway.  Even more so when I'm doing it in a car.
And while I've got you, imaginary version of Yacht  Club Games that only exists in my head, where is
the fucking logic in falling into deep water being  instant death, but BURROWING into deep water being
the intended traversal method? What, does Mina  also need a little hop delay to have time to
remind herself that she knows how to swim? Well,  I did figure that out myself when I was trapped in
an area that required me to do it to escape, but I  was trapped there long enough that my reaction to
the discovery was not so much "Aha!" as "NGHHH."  And then there's the mirrors. I kept finding
mirrors in the world that didn't seem to have any  function besides letting you make funny faces,
and I didn't figure out until it became necessary  to find the last generator that this is the fast
travel system I was supposed to have been  using the whole game. Turns out you can walk
into mirrors if you keep pushing at them, but it  takes a few seconds, and I don't know about you,
but when I bang my head into a wall one time and  my head fails to penetrate the wall, I kind of
assume my relationship with the wall is concluded.  But lucky you, viewer. You get to go into Mina the
Hollower already knowing that and can enjoy  a fun, sprawling, nostalgic adventure that's
breaking new ground as long as you can actually  penetrate it. Dig in. There's a lot going on
below the surface. Its favorite kind of coffee is  ground. I need to stop now; I've got a nosebleed.
